skill.name: gws-calendar-insert
skill.description: Google Calendar: Create a new event.
skill.summary: Google Calendar: Create a new event.
skill.author: googleworkspace

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged shell-style command examples, relative documentation links, and weak-crypto patterns. Review found no malicious intent, no prompt injection, and no cryptographic code; the real concern is that this skill instructs an external Google Workspace CLI to create calendar events, which is a write action requiring user confirmation.

medium_findings.title: External CLI Write Operation
medium_findings.description: The skill documents use of the gws CLI to create Google Calendar events. This is legitimate for the skill purpose, but it modifies a user's calendar and can invite attendees or add Meet links, so execution should require explicit user confirmation.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples directly invoke an external CLI for a write action. The file also explicitly warns that confirmation is required, which supports legitimate intent but confirms operational risk.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backticks Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The repeated Ruby or shell backtick alerts are false positives. The affected lines are Markdown inline code, a fenced bash usage block, a flag table, and command examples, not executable Ruby code inside the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the suspicious characters are documentation formatting. No Ruby code, dynamic evaluation, or hidden shell wrapper is present.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Misidentified as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The ../ references point to sibling skill documentation for shared Google Workspace guidance and related calendar commands. They do not construct paths from user input or access arbitrary files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The relative paths are static Markdown references and a prerequisite instruction to read shared documentation. There is no variable path construction or file write behavior.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Not Supported by Evidence
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm alerts are false positives. The cited lines contain the skill description, a Markdown table header, and a description flag; no hashing, encryption, or crypto API usage appears.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations do not contain cryptographic primitives or encoded payloads. They are ordinary front matter and documentation text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Calendar Event Creation Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The documented command creates Google Calendar events through the gws CLI. This is expected behavior but is a write operation against an external account.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command name and examples clearly describe event insertion. The risk is limited to intended calendar writes rather than arbitrary command execution.

content.value_statement: Scheduling work often requires precise event details, attendee lists, and optional video links. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code prepare Google Calendar event creation commands through the gws CLI.
actual_capabilities: Creates new Google Calendar events with a required summary, start time, and end time.
Supports the primary calendar by default and can target another calendar ID.
Adds optional event location and description fields.
Adds one or more attendee email addresses.
Can request a Google Meet video conference link for the event.
Documents required confirmation before running the calendar write command.
limitations: Requires the gws CLI and valid Google Workspace authentication from the shared setup.
Does not list calendar availability or detect scheduling conflicts.
Requires RFC3339 or ISO 8601 time values for reliable execution.
Creates events only after the user confirms the write action.

use_cases.title: Schedule a Team Meeting
use_cases.description: Create a calendar event with a clear title, start time, end time, attendees, and an optional Meet link.

use_cases.title: Add Focus Blocks
use_cases.description: Insert protected work sessions on a primary or named calendar with accurate start and end times.

use_cases.title: Prepare Customer Calls
use_cases.description: Create client-facing events that include a location, description, attendees, and video conferencing when needed.

prompt_templates.title: Create Basic Event
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request with only required fields.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Google Calendar event called Project Sync from 2026-07-02T15:00:00Z to 2026-07-02T15:30:00Z. Show me the command before running it.

prompt_templates.title: Add Attendees
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare a Google Calendar event named Design Review for 2026-07-03T10:00:00-07:00 to 2026-07-03T11:00:00-07:00 with alice@example.com and ben@example.com as attendees.

prompt_templates.title: Include Meeting Details
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a richer event with place and agenda.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a calendar event for Quarterly Planning on 2026-07-10T13:00:00-07:00 through 2026-07-10T14:30:00-07:00. Add the location Conference Room B and description Review roadmap priorities.

prompt_templates.title: Target Calendar With Meet
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request using optional calendar and video flags.
prompt_templates.prompt: Prepare the gws command to add Partner Kickoff to calendar partners@example.com from 2026-07-15T09:00:00-07:00 to 2026-07-15T10:00:00-07:00 with a Google Meet link and two attendees.

output_examples.input: Schedule Standup tomorrow from 9:00 to 9:30 with a Meet link.
output_examples.output: The assistant confirms the date, converts the time to an accepted timestamp, shows the calendar creation command, and asks for approval before execution.

output_examples.input: Add a Review meeting with Alice as an attendee.
output_examples.output: The assistant prepares an event creation command with the title, required start and end times, and Alice's attendee email.

output_examples.input: Create a planning block on my work calendar with a room location.
output_examples.output: The assistant targets the requested calendar, includes the location, and explains that the event will be created only after confirmation.

best_practices: Confirm the calendar ID, timezone, start time, and end time before creating the event.
Show the exact command and ask for user approval before running any write action.
Use full RFC3339 timestamps when scheduling across timezones.
anti_patterns: Do not create events from vague relative times without confirming the exact date and timezone.
Do not invite attendees until the user has approved the recipient list.
Do not run the write command when required title or time fields are missing.

faq.question: What does this skill create?
faq.answer: It creates a new Google Calendar event using the gws calendar insert command.

faq.question: Does it require authentication?
faq.answer: Yes. It depends on the shared Google Workspace authentication setup for the gws CLI.

faq.question: Can it add attendees?
faq.answer: Yes. The attendee flag can be used multiple times for multiple email addresses.

faq.question: Can it create a Google Meet link?
faq.answer: Yes. The meet flag requests a Google Meet conference link for the event.

faq.question: Does it check calendar availability?
faq.answer: No. This skill documents event creation and does not check conflicts or free-busy data.

faq.question: Is user confirmation required?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill identifies this as a write command and says to confirm before execution.
